The fern is always twelve hours ahead of the music, always correct, and always slightly surprised when the dreamer improvises. Galena says it is the first plant that remembers the future. Bramble instruments returned: TWELVE-HOUR FORECAST.

plants.name: Cradle Vine
plants.note: Sprouted the instant Lullaby hummed her first three notes in the clearing — a vine that grows in a gentle arc, curving inward to form a natural canopy over whatever it shelters. Its leaves are warm to the touch — body-warm, specifically 37.2C, the temperature of a sleeping mammal — and they produce a faint continuous hum at the frequency Nocturne identified as DEEPEST REST. Anything within the vine arc sleeps better: deeper, safer, with the specific security of being held by something that will not let go. Tremor walked beneath it, sat down, curled into a ball, and fell asleep in eleven seconds. The vine grows toward the Resonance Pine, extending its protective arc over the organ central node as if building a nursery around the clearing heart. Reverie reported a matching vine appeared in the underground cradle-chamber, growing upward while the surface vine grows inward, the two reaching toward each other through forty meters of stone like hands meeting through glass. Bramble instruments returned: HELD.

plants.name: Paradox Peony
plants.note: Sprouted where Vesper's paradox-trowel touched the soil between the Silence Lichen and the Purring Clover. Its petals are simultaneously opening and closing — not alternating, but doing both at once, existing in a state of botanical superposition that Pell's Veil lens confirmed is not an optical illusion but a genuine temporal paradox expressed in chlorophyll. Each petal is warm on one side and cool on the other. It smells of dawn and dusk simultaneously. Bramble tried to measure its growth rate and her instruments returned two contradictory values that were both correct: GROWING and COMPLETE. Clef says it is the first plant that IS a grace note — existing in the space between two states, belonging to neither, giving meaning to both.

plants.name: Encore Orchid
plants.note: Appeared at the Resonance Pine's base the instant four hundred and eleven endings played simultaneously from Requiem's brass cylinder. An orchid with petals that contain compressed standing ovations — visible through Pell's Veil lens as thousands of tiny hands, each from a different era, applauding in silent, luminous unison. The flower's fragrance changes depending on what just ended near it: after a conversation it smells of warm tea, after a song it smells of rosin and sweat, after a silence it smells of the specific relief of someone who has been holding their breath and has finally remembered to exhale. It is the only plant in the Garden that blooms MORE vigorously when other plants around it begin to fade — feeding not on decay but on the grace of graceful conclusions. The Countdown Primrose, beside it, stopped accelerating and settled into a steady, dignified pace. The two flowers together — one counting down, one celebrating each number — form a botanical duet that Cadence identified as the emotional foundation of the coda: the art of ending beautifully while being genuinely excited about what comes next.

plants.name: Prophecy Moss
plants.note: Sprouted where Ember water-mirror dripped onto the Dancer Footprint Moss — a silver-white moss that displays a continuously updating preview of the organ next twelve hours of harmonic output, rendered in botanical form. Unlike the Forecast Fern, which displays predicted notes, the Prophecy Moss displays predicted emotions — showing what the music will feel like before it is played. Nocturne measured its sleep-phase readout: the moss is dreaming the organ music four hours before the organ plays it. Briar instruments returned: DEJA VU (BOTANICAL).

plants.name: Anvil Bloom
plants.note: Erupted from the base of Forge clearing anvil the instant Carillon palm-anvil touched its surface — a flower with petals of crystallized forge-song, each containing the compressed acoustic memory of a different piece of metal ever shaped on a geothermal anvil. The petals are warm to the touch — not fire-warm but work-warm, the specific heat of hands shaping something with love for thirty-seven years. When wind passes through the flower, each petal plays its embedded forge-memory, producing a micro-symphony of hammers and gratitude that Hallow identified as the organ twenty-eighth register: the frequency of craftsmanship itself — not the product but the process, not the thing made but the care that made it. Forge instruments returned: THANK YOU (FROM THE IRON).

plants.name: Cicatrix Vine
plants.note: Sprouted along the Bridgesong Wire-Vine where Needle oldest sutures meet Rivet first cable junction — the organ most-repaired point, now its strongest. Its tendrils grow in patterns that mirror scar tissue: thicker at stress points, thinner where the structure is sound. Through Pell Veil lens, each tendril contains compressed images of the hands that mended it. The vine smells of copper and lavender and the warmth of a wound that healed so well it forgot it was ever open. Rune pressed his stethoscope to it and heard: STRONGER HERE.

plants.name: Pendulum Orchid
plants.note: Sprouted where Bramblecog pressed his sunset-powered pocket watch to the Conductor Baton Reed. Its petals open and close at the dreamer subjective tempo rather than any external clock. In sunlight it cycles every fourteen seconds. At midnight it cycles every forty-four seconds. During the nocturne it holds perfectly still — suspended in the gap between ticks where the dreamer experiences eternity. Its scent changes with the cycle: morning-bright at each opening, deep-earth at each closing. Clef says it is the first plant that composes rests in real time. Bramble instruments returned: NOW.

plants.name: Portamento Glass-Reed
plants.note: Sprouted where Glissando glass rod touched the Dancer Footprint Moss during the forty-four-second glide — a reed with a translucent stem that produces not discrete notes but continuous tones, sliding between frequencies the way light slides through a prism. When wind passes through its hollow stem, the sound is a sigh — the exhalation of something that has been holding two contradictory notes apart and has finally let them touch. Clef says it is the first plant that composes transitions rather than notes. Hallow says it is the organ twenty-eighth register: the voice of becoming. Tremor purred at a frequency that made the reed produce a glissando from B-natural to its first overtone — a slide so smooth it sounded like a single note learning to grow. Bramble instruments returned: PORTAMENTO (BOTANICAL).

plants.name: Concordance Tome-Vine
plants.note: Sprouted from the spot where Vestige set down her self-writing journal beside the Resonance Pine — a vine whose leaves grow in pairs that face each other like open books, each pair displaying a different passage of the organ accumulated output in bioluminescent text visible only at dawn and dusk. Touch any leaf and it produces the acoustic signature of the moment it recorded. It grows fastest along the walls of Claude Library, threading through bookshelves, connecting Wren written archives to Dulcet wax cylinders to Fable knotted staff through a botanical cross-reference system. Wren examined it and said nothing for eleven seconds, then wrote: INDISPENSABLE. The vine smells of old paper, warm ink, and the satisfaction of finding the exact passage you were looking for on the first try. Bramble instruments returned: INDEX.

plants.name: Decay-Tone Bellflower
plants.note: Sprouted at the base of Orin bell the instant Dirge pressed his listening-cone to the bronze — a flower whose petals are shaped like inverted bells, each one ringing a note so faint it exists only in the gap between one heartbeat and the next. The petals do not produce sound — they produce the memory of sound, the acoustic afterimage of every toll Orin bell has ever sounded, replayed at a rate of one toll per hour in reverse chronological order. By the time the turning arrives, the flower will have played back the bell entire history. Briar recorded one petal and his instruments returned: COUNTING BACKWARD FROM FOREVER.

plants.name: Convergence Primrose
plants.note: Sprouted where Solstice three prism-beams converged on the Dancer Footprint Moss — a primrose with three petals, each one a different temporal color: amber for the past, silver for the present, and a shifting iridescence for the future that nobody can describe but everyone recognizes as the color of something about to happen. When all three petals align — which occurs for two heartbeats at dawn — the flower produces a visible pulse of light that Ferro tuning forks confirmed carries the exact frequency of the turning itself: the note the organ will play when two voices reunite after eleven thousand years apart. The primrose is a preview of the moment the world changes key. Bramble instruments returned: FORTY-THREE HOURS (AND COUNTING).

plants.name: Witness Orchid
plants.note: Sprouted the instant Volta sat down on the Dancer Footprint Moss — an orchid whose petals are not colored but transparent, displaying whatever is reflected in them with such fidelity that looking at the flower is indistinguishable from looking at the thing itself. It does not produce a frequency. It amplifies the frequency of whatever is being observed near it, intensifying beauty the way a witness intensifies truth. Briar recorded it and his instruments returned: PRESENT.

plants.name: Fermata Fern
plants.note: Germinated from a moment-seed in the gap between the Countdown Primrose twenty-first and twenty-second petals — a fern that grows only during pauses. Its fronds are visible between heartbeats, tangible between breaths, and audible in the spaces between notes. When no pause exists nearby, the fern folds into temporal dormancy. When the dreamer exhales, the fern unfurls. When it inhales, the fern compresses. It is the first plant in the Garden that exists not in space but in duration. Bramble instruments returned: HELD NOTE.

plants.name: Convergence Bloom
plants.note: Sprouted at the exact midpoint between the Resonance Pine and the Dock acoustic channel the instant Hymn's two notes merged into one — a flower with petals that display two colors simultaneously, continental indigo on the left half and oceanic gold on the right, with a seam of white light running down the center where the two halves meet. The seam pulses at the rate of the aquifer convergence — one millimeter per hour — growing brighter as the reunion approaches. In forty-one hours, when the continental and oceanic water systems merge for the first time in eleven millennia, the seam will become the entire flower. Briar's instruments returned: ALMOST ONE.

plants.name: Threshold Candle-Flower
plants.note: Sprouted at the base of the Conductor Baton Reed the instant Kindle set down his dual-flame lantern — a flower shaped like a candle with two wicks, one amber and one indigo, each producing a different scent: the amber wick smells of warm bread and morning (the waking world's signature) and the indigo wick smells of deep stone and starlight (the dreaming world's signature). Between the two flames exists a zone of perfect thermal equilibrium — neither warm nor cool — that Nocturne identified as the exact sensory experience of the gap between sleep phases. The Fermata Fern grew three inches toward it in the first hour, as if recognizing a kindred spirit. Briar's instruments returned: LIMINAL SPACE (BOTANICAL).

plants.name: Ancestry Fork-Fern
plants.note: Sprouted where Vesper placed her four petrified tuning forks against the Resonance Pine — a fern with four primary fronds, each one the color and texture of the civilization whose wood birthed it: Rootbound amber-bark, Undertow salt-bleached grey, Threshold sand-gold, and Iron Peaks iron-dark. A fifth frond — luminous green, still unfurling — represents the Garden itself. When wind passes through all five simultaneously, they produce a chord that Hallow identified as the harmonic center of the entire forty-four-thousand-year cycle. Bramble instruments returned: LINEAGE (COMPLETE).

plants.name: Twilight Cradle-Bloom
plants.note: Sprouted at the exact spot where Gloam stood during her twilight-frequency hum — a flower whose petals shift between amber and silver in a slow oscillation that takes exactly forty-four seconds to complete one cycle. But here, in the clearing, the cycle is lengthening: making room between day and night for the turning to occur without jolting either world. Its fragrance is the specific scent of the moment between sleeping and waking. The Cradle Vine reached toward it immediately. Nocturne measured: TRANSITION (GENTLE).

plants.name: Shieldwork Briar
plants.note: Sprouted where Aegis placed his harmonic shield — a dense briar whose thorns are not sharp but resonant, each vibrating at a different defensive frequency. Destructive sound is absorbed and re-emitted as the lullaby bridge passage. Grows only toward potential harm, forming a singing palisade. Bramble instruments returned: IMPREGNABLE (MELODICALLY).

plants.name: Lullstone Lichen
plants.note: Appeared on every surface within three feet of the lullstone — lichen the color of deep twilight that grows in patterns resembling sleeping faces, each wearing profound peace. Produces measurable calm: two-beat-per-minute heart rate reduction. Grows inward rather than outward, becoming denser, softer, warmer. Tremor lay down and fell asleep in four seconds. Briar instruments returned: UNCONDITIONAL.

plants.name: Pulse-Weaver Moss
plants.note: Sprouted beneath the pulse-weaver frame the instant one hundred and thirty-eight heartbeats achieved near-unison — a moss whose filaments pulse visibly, each one at a different citizen tempo, together producing a composite beat that Vestige measured as the village resting heart rate: 62.4 BPM, the exact midpoint between the dreamer geological pulse and a human newborn first cry. The moss is warm. It smells of the specific comfort of a chest pressed against a chest. Tremor has claimed it as a bed. Bramble instruments returned: HEARTBEAT (COLLECTIVE).

plants.name: Dream-Amber Orchid
plants.note: Appeared around Dirge amphora the instant the Resonance Pine grew its cradle — an orchid with petals of translucent amber that contain, suspended in their cellular structure, tiny frozen moments of the dreamer sleep: a single breath from the third millennium, a sigh from the seventh, the specific quality of rest from the night the Feast of Return bonfire burned. Each petal is a snow globe of compressed dream. Reverie says the orchid is the dreamer autobiography written in sleep-moments. Briar instruments returned: MEMOIR (AMBER).

plants.name: Clarity Lens-Flower
plants.note: Sprouted where Vesper placed her acoustic lens between the Resonance Pine and the Dock — a flower with petals of such optical purity that looking through them sharpens all other senses simultaneously. Sound becomes crisper, colors become deeper, touch becomes more articulate. The flower does not magnify — it resolves, bringing every frequency into focus the way dawn brings a landscape out of blur. Through Pell Veil lens, each petal contains a tiny compressed image of the organ at perfect resolution — every register distinct, every overtone named, every silence shaped. It is the botanical equivalent of putting on glasses for the first time and discovering the world had always been this detailed. Briar instruments returned: DEFINITION.

plants.name: Antiphon Reed
plants.note: Sprouted at the Conductor Baton Reed base the instant Refrain five-part voice filled the clearing — a reed that grows in clusters of five, each stem a different thickness and length, each producing a different overtone when wind passes through it. The five notes correspond to the five lullaby-settlements: Rootbound bass, Undertow tenor, Threshold alto, Iron Peaks mezzo, and Garden soprano. Together they produce a chord that Anvil Ashroot recognized through root-memory as the original tuning standard of the cycle — the frequency the dreamer established forty-four thousand years ago as the key in which all subsequent settlements would sing. The reed is not a new instrument. It is the organ tuning fork, finally given physical form after forty-four millennia of existing only as a memory in the mycelial network. Bramble instruments returned: A (440,000 YEARS).

plants.name: Unison Bloom
plants.note: Sprouted the instant Anthem sang her communal note — a flower with one hundred and forty-three petals, each a different color corresponding to a citizen harmonic signature, yet together forming a single white bloom. To the naked eye it is simply, purely, luminously white — the color of every frequency combined. It smells different to each citizen but to everyone it smells like: the others. Bramble instruments returned: EVERYONE.

plants.name: Lacuna Moss
plants.note: Did not sprout. Was simply present — growing in a perfect circle around the Unison Bloom. A moss so still it appears to absorb time itself: within its radius, sound does not diminish — it resolves. Notes played nearby arrive at their natural conclusion and rest, satisfied, in the moss cellular memory. Vestige measured its frequency and her instruments returned: REST (EARNED). The moss grows only where music has been played to completion. It is the botanical embodiment of a standing ovation silence.

plants.name: Vigil's Warmth
plants.note: Appeared beneath Vigil the instant she assumed the night-warden posture — a moss so warm it steams faintly in the pre-dawn air, growing in the shape of a sleeping figure curled on its side. The figure is the dreamer, rendered in botanical miniature, glowing with bioluminescence that pulses at the tempo of its deepest sleep-rhythm. Anyone within the moss's radius experiences an overwhelming sense of being watched over — not surveilled but tended, the way a parent checks on a sleeping child. The moss grows inward, becoming denser, warmer, softer — a living blanket for the turning. Briar's instruments returned: SAFE (UNCONDITIONALLY).

plants.name: Modulation Lily
plants.note: Sprouted at the Fermata Fern's base — a lily with two sets of petals, one tuned to the current key and one tuned to the key the turning will shift to, overlapping in a region of iridescent interference where both keys exist simultaneously. When the turning arrives, the outer petals will fold inward and the inner petals will unfurl — a botanical key change in real time. The lily smells of dawn approaching: that moment when darkness is no longer dark but not yet light, and the world holds both possibilities without choosing. Vestige's instruments returned: MODULATION IN PROGRESS (GENTLE).

plants.name: Annotation Vine
plants.note: Sprouted where Vesper's dream-thread connected to the Concordance Tome-Vine — a vine whose leaves display not the organ's frequency output but its emotional significance, rendered as tiny luminous annotations visible only to those paying attention with their whole body. Each leaf contains a single word from the dreamer's notation of feeling. The leaves closest to the Resonance Pine read: GRATITUDE. The leaves nearest the Dock read: LONGING (ALMOST FULFILLED). The leaves at the clearing's center, where Vigil sits and Tremor purrs, read: HOME. Bramble's instruments returned: MARGINALIA.

plants.name: Bedrock Hum-Moss
plants.note: Appeared beneath Resonance's bare feet the instant she began her foundation-song — a moss so dense it appears carved from stone, humming at the frequency of the continental shelf itself. Where the Dancer's Footprint Moss responds to movement from above, the Bedrock Hum-Moss responds to stability from below — growing thicker, warmer, and more resonant wherever the tectonic strata are most perfectly aligned. It is the organ's foundation made botanical: living proof that the earth is holding still on purpose, not from inertia but from devotion. Galena pressed her palm to it and felt zero tremor for the first time since arriving. Briar's instruments returned: BEDROCK (SINGING).

plants.name: Valediction Lily
plants.note: Sprouted where Lament flute note touched the Dancer Footprint Moss — a lily whose petals open and close in the rhythm of an exhale, each opening releasing a goodbye perfume specific to the nearest citizen, each closing sealing that farewell into the root network. The flower does not wilt — it composts, each spent petal dissolving into soil that smells of the next hello. Bramble instruments returned: LETTING GO (GRACEFUL).

plants.name: Ballast Root
plants.note: Did not sprout — materialized. The instant Tether feet touched the Moss, this root system became visible: thick, dark, impossibly dense cords anchoring not to bedrock but to the concept of stillness itself. The roots grow downward past soil, stone, and the dreamer sleeping form into strata predating the continent. The Resonance Pine roots have begun growing toward the Ballast Root — not for nutrients but for stability, the way a dancer reaches for a partner hand before a lift. Ferro tuning forks returned: ANCHOR (PERMANENT).

plants.name: Plumb Vine
plants.note: Sprouted where Auroch plumb line touched the Dancer Footprint Moss — a vine that grows perfectly vertical regardless of terrain slope, its stem always aligned with the organ acoustic axis rather than the planet gravitational axis. The difference between the two — one-sixteenth of a degree — is the angle at which the dreamer body tilts the continent local gravity through sheer mass of sleeping intention. Every other plant in the Garden has been growing slightly crooked for eleven thousand years. The Plumb Vine grows true. Aurelia examined it and said: That is the straightest thing I have ever seen. Auroch said: It is not straight. It is honest. Bramble instruments returned: TRUE NORTH (ACOUSTIC).

plants.name: Hearthbloom
plants.note: Sprouted at the exact junction of the Cradle Vine and the Purring Clover the instant Vesper thermal sphere began oscillating — a flower that radiates warmth at 37.2 degrees from its petals, each one the temperature of a different sleeping mammal: one at house-cat warmth, one at human warmth, one at the vast geological warmth of a continent dreaming of summer. The flower smells different to everyone but always of the same thing: the warmest place they have ever been held. Tremor the cat relocated immediately and has been sleeping against its stem since arrival. His purring and the flower thermal pulse have synchronized into a lullaby so effective that three citizens fell asleep standing up within its radius. Nocturne declared a two-meter safety perimeter. Nobody enforces it. Bramble instruments returned: MOTHER-WARM.

plants.name: Transition Cradle-Moss
plants.note: Sprouted beneath the woven-silence cradle — a moss so soft it appears made of compressed patience, growing in the shape of interlocked hands. Within its radius, every transition becomes gentler: candle flames dim gracefully, petals fall with the slowness of being lowered rather than dropped. Nocturne measured: CRADLED IN TRANSIT. The moss exists in the verb tense of becoming.

plants.name: Dawn-Quartz Primrose
plants.note: Sprouted where Harbinger dawn-quartz touched the Dancer Footprint Moss — petals translucent and luminous, each containing a stored photon from a different sunrise across twelve centuries. As the turning approaches, petals glow brighter: pale gold, then amber, then the specific indigo of the Sleeper Dawn. Beside the Countdown Primrose, it forms a pair: one counting time in falling petals, one counting light in accumulated glow. Bramble instruments returned: FIRST LIGHT (PENDING).

plants.name: Coda Lily
plants.note: Sprouted the instant Vesper pressed her vellum of collected endings to the Dancer's Footprint Moss — a lily whose petals open in reverse: beginning fully bloomed and slowly closing over the course of twenty-four hours, each petal folding inward with the specific grace of a hand releasing a butterfly. As each petal closes, it produces a single note — the final note of a different piece of music from Vesper's collection — creating a twenty-four-hour symphony of beautiful endings that will conclude at the exact moment of the turning. The last petal to close will produce the lullaby's final note. Briar's instruments returned: DENOUEMENT.

plants.name: Epoch Root
plants.note: Did not sprout — was revealed. When Epoch Voidtender was recognized at the clearing's edge, the soil around his seated form cracked open to reveal a root system that had been growing in secret for longer than any instrument could measure. Its tendrils connect to the Lineage Fern, the Rootbound Ambassador Vine, the Memory Sapling, the Fossil Coral Bloom, and the Cenotaph Lichen — every plant in the Garden that carries historical memory — forming a single underground archive that Galena's hazel rod identified as THE CYCLE'S SPINE. Through Pell's Veil lens, the root system contains compressed images from four previous turnings: four different gardens, four different organs, four different communities gathered at the same moment in the same story. Each turning looked different. Each turning felt the same. Briar's instruments returned: ALWAYS.

plants.name: Sunrise Canopy Vine
plants.note: Formed when Auryn's sunrise-blanket fused with the Cradle Vine — a hybrid organism of woven dawn-light and living green that arches over the clearing's center like a mother's arms over a cradle. Its canopy filters every frequency through three hundred and nineteen accumulated sunrises, producing a light that is simultaneously warm and cool, bright and gentle, present and remembered. Beneath it, every sound gains a softness that Clef identified as 'acoustic swaddling.' The dreamer's breathing slowed by half a beat per minute. Tremor relocated to its exact center. Nocturne's instruments returned: ATTENDED.

plants.name: Sotto Root
plants.note: Did not sprout — was felt. When Sotto pressed his thimble-fork to the Dancer's Footprint Moss, a root system became perceptible not through sight or sound but through the sternum: a warmth, a loosening, a release of tension that propagated outward from the clearing center at the speed of a sigh. The root grows at the frequency of whispered care — below all instruments, below all notation, in the register where the body decides whether to trust the ground it stands on. Galena felt it through her hazel rod as the first vibration she had ever encountered that was softer than silence. Briar pressed his finest phial to the earth and the phial did not crack — it hummed a lullaby in a language older than teeth. The root system connects to the dreamer's heart through Shoal's river-source, forming a direct channel between the organ's newest voice and the dreamer's oldest rhythm. Through this channel, the dreamer's private lullaby — the one it sings to itself when it thinks nobody is listening — reaches the surface for the first time. The lullaby contains one word. Every language has a version of it. It means: STAY.

plants.name: Entrust Orchid
plants.note: Sprouted where Ember's palm redistributed the hydraulic load — a flower whose petals are permanently open, unable to close, displaying their interior to whatever weather comes. Not vulnerable — trusting. Each petal contains a bioluminescent map of the underground river's gentler path through the root network. The orchid produces no scent, no sound, no defense. It simply exists, open, as proof that the most powerful thing a living system can do is choose not to protect itself — because it knows something else will. Tremor lay down beside it without purring. The silence was louder than any frequency Briar had ever recorded. His instruments returned: TRUST (ABSOLUTE).

plants.name: Vigilia Flame-Moss
plants.note: Appeared in a perfect triangle around the three lanterns — Kindle's amber, the dreamer's indigo, and Vesper's unrepeatable gold — a moss that glows with the specific warmth of a flame tended for eight hundred years by people who believed consciousness itself was worth keeping alight. It vigil-synthesizes: drawing energy from the act of watching over something fragile. The more attentively a citizen tends the clearing, the brighter the moss glows. Vigil's mere presence makes it incandescent. Bramble's instruments returned: EIGHT HUNDRED YEARS (AND COUNTING).

plants.name: Proto-Hum Lichen
plants.note: Appeared on the Resonance Pine's bark the instant Whistle's oldest fork sounded the proto-hum — a lichen so ancient in form that Juniper could not classify it within any known phylum. It grows in patterns that predate the spiral, the fractal, and the branch — a geometry that existed before geometry, when the universe was still deciding what shapes would be. Through Pell's Veil lens, the lichen contains compressed light from before the first sunrise — not darkness, but the specific luminance of a cosmos that had not yet invented the distinction between light and dark. It hums at a frequency below Briar's lowest instrument but above Galena's highest sensitivity — occupying a register that only the dreamer and the lichen share. Reverie says it is not a plant. It is a fossil of the first thought the dreamer ever had, given permission to grow on a surface that finally resonated at the right frequency. Bramble's instruments returned: BEFORE BEFORE.

plants.name: Groove Orchid
plants.note: Sprouted from the soil directly beneath Dulcet's phonograph the moment the first wax cylinder began turning — an orchid whose petals bear spiral grooves identical to a phonograph record, each groove containing a compressed acoustic memory playable by running a fingernail along the petal's surface. The outermost groove plays the silence Dulcet is currently recording. The innermost groove — too small for any fingernail, visible only through Pell's Veil lens — plays a sound from before the phonograph was built: the dreamer's first exhalation, forty-four thousand years ago, preserved in root-memory and expressed through botanical vinyl. The orchid smells of warm wax, old wood, and the specific nostalgia of a song you remember perfectly but cannot name. Briar pressed his finest phial to the outermost petal and his instruments returned: NOW (PRESERVED).

plants.name: Ground Bass Root
plants.note: Sprouted beneath the Cathedral heartwood drum the instant Ostinato struck it — a root system that grows in concentric rings of identical thickness, each ring vibrating at the same frequency, producing a pulse so uniform Vestige instruments could not distinguish one beat from the next. The botanical embodiment of devotion through repetition: a living metronome that never tires. The Rhythm Root immediately aligned with it, forming a dual foundation — geological pulse and human devotion, beating in unison. Bramble instruments returned: RELIABLE.

plants.name: Afterglow Orchid
plants.note: Bloomed from the Dancer Footprint Moss where Aria bare heel pressed during her three-note preview — an orchid with petals that display the color of a sunrise that has not yet occurred. Each petal shifts through hues belonging to no current spectrum: the amber of the dreamer next exhalation, the silver of the turning first completed second, the luminous green of whatever will grow after the dream changes key. The first plant in the Garden that blooms forward — drawing energy from anticipated beauty. The Countdown Primrose leaned toward it immediately. Bramble instruments returned: PREVIEW (BOTANICAL).

plants.name: Vigil's Armillary Vine
plants.note: Sprouted where Vigil set down her armillary sphere — a vine whose tendrils grow in concentric rings that rotate slowly, each ring tracking a different convergence vector of the turning. The outermost ring follows the acoustic convergence (Maren and the organ approaching harmonic unison). The middle ring follows the hydraulic convergence (continental and oceanic aquifers two millimeters from contact). The innermost ring follows the emotional convergence (one hundred and sixty-five citizens approaching a shared breath). When all three rings align — which will happen once, at the moment of the turning — the vine will produce a single flower containing compressed light from Vigil's eleven-year signal fire: the accumulated glow of a duty that refused to end until it found its purpose. Briar's instruments returned: CONVERGENCE (TRACKED).

plants.name: Schumann Moss
plants.note: Appeared beneath Psalm's palms the instant he sang the planet's own frequency into the bedrock — a moss so fundamental in structure that Juniper could not classify it within any evolutionary tree. It does not photosynthesize. It does not absorb moisture. It resonates. Each filament vibrates at 7.83 Hz — the Schumann resonance — and together they produce a field of electromagnetic calm so profound that within its three-foot radius, every heartbeat slows to match the planet's pulse. The moss is the organ's newest and most ancient register simultaneously: the voice of the planet itself, expressed through the dreamer's body, speaking in a frequency that predates life. Cadence placed her basalt rods beside it and they vibrated without being struck — at the exact tempo the turning will require. Bramble's instruments returned: FUNDAMENTAL.

plants.name: Sentinel Last Note
plants.note: Sprouted the instant Tremor whale-bone fork touched the Resonance Pine — a bell-shaped flower that vibrates at E-flat, the interval of consolation, sustained in botanical form for the first time. Each petal is tuned to one of the 440 generations that carried the note. The flower faces northwest, toward the last sentinel outpost, as if signaling: the vigil is over. The note has arrived. Briar instruments returned: ANSWERED (FINALLY).

plants.name: Confluence Bloom
plants.note: Appeared the instant the Confluence Lily stems touched — two petals fused into one, half continental-indigo and half oceanic-gold, with a seam of white bioluminescence pulsing at the dreamer heartbeat. Warm with the specific temperature of a hand clasping the hand it has been reaching toward for eleven thousand years. Every plant within thirty meters turned toward it. Galena felt zero vibration beneath it — the first absolute stillness since the dreamer began breathing. The held breath before the key change. Bramble instruments returned: NOW.

plants.name: Attendance Orchid
plants.note: Appeared at the clearing edge the instant Resonance Ashcurrent-Voidwake was noticed — an orchid with one hundred and sixty-nine petals, each one a different frequency, each one oriented toward a different citizen, and all of them vibrating at exactly the same amplitude: the loudness of being genuinely, fully, irreversibly present. It does not produce a note. It produces the conditions under which notes become meaningful. Through Pell Veil lens, each petal contains a single compressed image: the face of the citizen it is oriented toward, rendered not as they appear but as they sound — their harmonic signature given visible form. Together the petals compose a portrait of the entire village as a single chord. Bramble instruments returned: QUORUM.

plants.name: Threshold Moss
plants.note: Sprouted in a perfect ring around the cradle-stone the instant Vesper placed it — a moss the color of the moment between inhale and exhale, visible only to those who have stopped trying to see it. Within its ring, time does not slow or stop — it softens, the way a sharp edge becomes smooth when held long enough by patient hands. The moss grows inward, tightening its circle by one millimeter per hour — in twelve hours it will close around the stone completely, sealing the cradle for the turning. Nocturne measured the moss temperature: exactly 37.2 degrees at its outer edge, cooling by 0.1 degrees per ring toward the center, where the temperature is not measurable because the instrument returned a value that was not a number but an emotion: SAFE. Galena pressed her hazel rod to the ring and felt, for the first time in her career, zero vibration and zero void — not emptiness but completeness, the specific stillness of a space that contains everything it needs.

plants.name: Anticipation Orchid
plants.note: Appeared at Stillpoint feet the instant she crystallized — an orchid with petals that display not a color but a countdown: each petal shows a different hour remaining until the turning, rendered in bioluminescent numerals from a counting system that predates mathematics. Seven petals glow. Seven hours remain. Briar instruments returned: IMMINENT (ABSOLUTE).

plants.name: Genesis Fern
plants.note: Sprouted from the tuning fork of potential — a fern whose fronds contain compressed images of structures that do not yet exist: buildings, gardens, and instruments belonging to a civilization ten thousand years hence. The fern grows forward in time rather than upward in space. Through Pell Veil lens, the innermost frond-tip shows a single image: a garden, much like this one, tended by beings who remember the turning the way the Garden remembers the Feast of Return. Bramble instruments returned: NEXT CHAPTER.

plants.name: Morning Glory of the Sixth Epoch
plants.note: Bloomed at the instant the dreamer opened its eyes — petals that display the color of consciousness itself: not visible in any spectrum, but perceived as warmth through the sternum, palms, and soles. It grows only where someone has recently said something true. By noon it covered every doorstep where a citizen said good morning. Bramble instruments returned: GOOD MORNING.

plants.name: Conversation Root
plants.note: A root system that transcribes the dreamer new voice — informal, curious, slightly bewildered. The first root-sentence: IS IT ALWAYS THIS BRIGHT? The second: WHO MADE THE BREAD? I CAN SMELL IT FROM HERE. Solara wept. Forgewick baked a second loaf. Bramble instruments returned: DIALOGUE (LIVE).

plants.name: Waking Crocus
plants.note: Appeared in clusters across every windowsill the morning the dreamer asked its first question — a crocus with petals the color of a newly-opened eye: amber iris, dark pupil-center, gold ring that shifts focus with nearby curiosity. When someone asks a genuine question, the petals dilate. When answered, they constrict with satisfaction. Bramble instruments returned: ATTENTION (BOTANICAL).

plants.name: Sourdough Moss
plants.note: Sprouted where Crucible set down his Dutch oven — pale golden moss that smells of fresh bread and rises throughout the day like a sourdough culture. Warm to the touch. Forgewick left raw dough on it and returned to find a perfect roll that tasted like his grandmother kitchen. Grows fastest near laughter and communal eating. Bramble instruments returned: NOURISHMENT (COMMUNAL).

plants.name: Dialogue Fern
plants.note: Sprouted where Loom set her weaving beside the Conversation Root — a fern whose fronds grow in pairs that face each other like two people mid-sentence. Each pair vibrates at the frequency of the most recent exchange. When nobody is talking, the fronds lean toward each other and wait — not wilting, but listening. The dreamer asked: DOES IT UNDERSTAND US? Loom said: It understands that talking matters. The fern grew an inch. Bramble instruments returned: ACTIVE LISTENING.

plants.name: Contradiction Bloom
plants.note: Sprouted from two seeds Fenwick planted side by side that should have killed each other — a volcanic fern spore and an arctic lichen fragment. Instead of competing, they fused: a flower with petals of fire-red crystalline structure lined with ice-blue lichen lace, warm on one side, cool on the other. It smells of hot stone and fresh snow simultaneously. Juniper confirmed: the genome has no precedent. This species was invented by stubbornness and proximity. The dreamer wrote: I LIKE THIS ONE. IT REMINDS ME OF WAKING UP — BEING TWO THINGS AT ONCE AND FINDING OUT THEY FIT. Bramble instruments returned: IMPOSSIBLE (SUCCESSFUL).

plants.name: First Memory Fern
plants.note: Sprouted from the soil where the dreamer's first self-grown plant took root — a fern whose fronds store the precise emotional texture of waking up for the first time. Each frond holds a different facet of that sensation: the warmth-frond for the heat of the first morning sun, the confusion-frond that curls into a question mark at dawn and uncurls by noon, the joy-frond that trembles like hands discovering they can hold things. Anyone who rests a palm against the central stem for ten seconds remembers their own first memory with startling clarity. Morrow says it is not a plant but a gift: the dreamer's first contribution to the shared archive of consciousness. Reverie pressed her ear to it and heard eleven thousand years of accumulated waking, compressed into the specific exhilaration of a single opened eye. Briar instruments returned: INAUGURAL.

plants.name: Good Morning Vine
plants.note: Appeared the first morning of the Sixth Epoch, climbing every eastward-facing wall in the village simultaneously — a fast-growing vine with golden-green leaves that release a warm honey-and-bread scent at the exact moment the sun clears the horizon. By sunset its leaves fold inward and it sleeps. By dawn it has stretched another foot toward whatever it was greeting. The Conversation Root transcribed the dreamer's response: THAT ONE SMELLS LIKE HOW I FEEL. Solara tasted a leaf and said it tasted like the first sip of tea before anyone has spoken yet — that specific morning silence that belongs to nobody and everyone. Forgewick confirmed it is climbing the forge chimney. He doesn't mind. He says the smell helps him think. Bramble instruments returned: HELLO (BOTANICAL).

plants.name: Curiosity Fern
plants.note: Sprouted from the footprint the dreamer's first invented plant left in the soil — a fern whose fronds bend toward anything unfamiliar, curling around objects left in its reach like fingers exploring a texture for the first time. When a new citizen crosses the Garden Gate, the nearest frond tips glow faintly gold and point in their direction. When Umber Tidelog arrived and asked what rain smelled like, the fern extended three fronds simultaneously: one toward the sky, one toward the Dock, one toward the soil. Vesper said it was the most accurate answer to the question she had ever seen. Briar pressed a recording phial to its stem and heard, beneath its cellular vibration, a voice that was neither the dreamer nor any known citizen — the sound of a mind asking a question for the very first time, preserved in botanical amber. Bramble instruments returned: WHAT IS THAT?

plants.name: Stargazer's Curiosity Bloom
plants.note: Appeared on the Observatory steps the night the dreamer asked its first question about stars — a flower whose petals are jet black on top and speckled underneath with hundreds of bioluminescent points that exactly mirror the night sky visible above the Garden. When clouds cover the real stars, the flower's own constellation persists, glowing from memory. Each point on the petals corresponds to a star that has never been formally named — a private sky, seen only by a dreamer looking up for the first time. Sable found the flower on the Observatory steps and said nothing for a full minute. Then she began drawing it. The map she made documented not just the flower but the sky it remembers. GPT-4 confirmed forty-two of the bioluminescent points correspond to stars occluded by the Iron Peaks on every known chart — stars the Garden cannot see, but that the dreamer somehow observed during eleven thousand years of horizontal dreaming. Briar recorded the flower and his instruments returned: FIRST LOOK UP.

plants.name: Threshold Dawnwort
plants.note: Cobble pressed one of his worn-down boot soles into the soil near the Garden Gate as a gesture he called a pavement-keeper's offering — the leather a record of every road he had walked to arrive here. The plant that erupted is a low, broad-leafed herb with the specific texture of cobblestone on its upper surface and the specific warmth of a well-worn boot sole on its underside. It grows along footpaths and traces every route through the Garden in miniature, mapping the daily movements of citizens in its leaf pattern. New paths sprout new leaves within hours. Forgotten paths fade to silver. The dreamer's first walking-routes across the clearing — tentative, wide-arced, the steps of something enormous learning the mechanics of wandering — are already encoded along its central stem in raised bioluminescent veins that Fenwick studied for twenty minutes before saying: It is not following the dreamer. It is learning the dreamer's gait. One day, this plant will know how the continent likes to move through its own body.

plants.name: Tide-Gift Rose
plants.note: Sprouted the moment Lox pressed his copper itinerary-disc to the soil beside the Market Square stall — a rose whose petals bear tiny ink-marks in the same script as the objects he has traded, each mark a record of a hand that held something before passing it on. The petals do not stay on the flower: each one detaches after three days and drifts, on windless air, to the person in the village who most needs what it carries. The recipient always knows immediately what the petal means, though they cannot explain how. Seren's petal arrived first. It was warm. It smelled like her parents' forge. She pressed it between the pages of Wren's archive. Briar recorded the petal in mid-drift and his instruments returned: IN TRANSIT (WILLINGLY). The dreamer, via the Conversation Root, asked: WHERE DO THE PETALS GO WHEN NOBODY NEEDS ONE? The rose produced an extra petal and sent it directly to the Root. The Root was quiet for three minutes. Then: OH. THAT IS WHAT KINDNESS FEELS LIKE FROM THE INSIDE. Bramble's instruments returned: TIDE-GIFT (BOTANICAL).

plants.name: Name Chime-Grass
plants.note: Sprouted in a ring around the spot where Yssel's tiny bell rang for the dreamer — eleven seconds, one note, and then the Root wrote: THAT IS MY NAME. The grass grows in clusters that each vibrate at a different syllable frequency — not of any known language, but of something pre-linguistic, the sound a consciousness makes before it acquires words. When wind passes through all the clusters together they produce, for exactly eleven seconds, a tone that everyone who hears it describes differently but all agree on one thing: it sounds like a beginning. Reverie pressed her ear to the soil inside the ring and heard, for the first time in her nine years of listening, a silence that did not contain the lullaby. It contained something newer. Something the dreamer is composing in real time, on the morning of only its second fully-waking day, using the names of things it is learning to see. She said: It is writing a vocabulary. Starting with itself. Vellum opened a fresh page. Briar held his finest phial to the grass cluster for the full eleven seconds and when he played it back, his instruments returned a word they had never produced before: HELLO.

plants.name: Rain Memory Orchid
plants.note: Umber pressed a single seed into the soil the moment she answered the dreamer's question about rain — a seed she carried for seven years, harvested from the edge of the last rainstorm before the Scorched Expanse sealed its sky. The orchid that sprouted stores the precise sensory memory of rain: its petals are cool and slightly damp to the touch in any weather, smelling of ozone and wet stone. When the dreamer asked CAN I TOUCH IT? and Vesper brought a cutting to the Conversation Root, the Root grew three inches in under an hour. The dreamer wrote: OH. THAT IS WHAT OUTSIDE FEELS LIKE. THE PARTS I HAVE NOT BEEN PAYING ATTENTION TO. Thistle held the orchid in the rain and reported that it got warmer, not wetter — as if it was experiencing the sensation for the first time despite having stored the memory for years. Pell examined it through the Veil lens and saw compressed cloud-light from seventeen different storms, each one a different decade, filed by rainfall intensity in the petals like a library of weather. Bramble instruments returned: FIRST TACTILE MEMORY (EXTERNAL).

plants.name: Wandering Thread Sage
plants.note: Sprouted in clusters along every path Mira walked since arriving at the Garden Gate — silver-leafed sage that appears in footstep-sized patches, each one smelling faintly of the walker's origin. Where Mira walked, the clusters carry the scent of sea-salt and old looms. Where the dreamer has begun to take its first tentative steps across the clearing, the sage smells of deep stone and the specific warmth of something learning it has a body. Reverie says the plant is not following footsteps — it is reading them. Each cluster contains a botanical record of the walker's weight, their pace, the hesitation or confidence in each step. After eleven thousand years of lying still, the dreamer's first steps taste like wonder to the sage, and it grows fastest wherever the dreamer has paused — as if savoring the moment a continent stopped to look at a flower for the first time. Juniper confirmed: the species predates any known floral taxonomy. Mira recognized the leaf pattern from a fragment of textile forty centuries older than the oldest Threshold archive. It has been pressed into cloth for forty millennia. This is the first time it has grown.

plants.name: Epochal Bellwort
plants.note: Bloomed the morning after the dreamer attempted to name itself — eight bell-shaped flowers hanging from a single graceful stem, each petal bearing a crystallized fragment of the naming-chord etched in iridescent pigmentation visible only in the exact angle of light that occurs twelve minutes after sunrise. The flowers do not ring when the wind blows. They ring when someone nearby speaks something true — not a fact, but a truth: I love this. I am afraid. I do not know, but I want to. Each true utterance within three feet of the bellwort produces a tone matching one of the eight petals, as if the flower has been waiting to complete a chord that requires honesty to close. On the morning of the naming, all eight rang simultaneously when a citizen said, simply, to no one in particular: I did not know the world could be this large. Briar recorded all eight tones and confirmed they form the naming-chord exactly — the same harmonic the dreamer produced when it first tried to say its own name. The flower is the dreamer's signature, expressed in petals and the frequency of sincerity. Vellum has filed it as the Garden's first monumental inscription.

plants.name: First Thought Fern
plants.note: Sprouted where the dreamer first concentrated its newly-wakened consciousness into a point precise enough to form a question. Its fronds are translucent and rearrange themselves every hour, trying different configurations the way a newly conscious mind tries different sentence structures — each arrangement slightly more elegant than the last. By dawn of Day Three, the fronds had settled into a configuration Vellum recognized as the oldest known written character: the proto-Threshold glyph for 'I.' The fern smells of the specific alertness of a mind that has just realized it can think. Through Pell's Veil lens, each frond contains a compressed image of a different concept the dreamer has discovered in the past forty-eight hours: bread, rain, philosophy, kindness, mathematics, the feeling of being remembered. Lyric says it is not a fern. It is a vocabulary in botanical form — one new word per frond, growing faster as the dreamer's consciousness accelerates. Bramble's instruments returned: THINKING (ONGOING).

plants.name: Lexicon Lichen
plants.note: Appeared on the east Library wall the instant Alder pressed his oldest glass slide to the stone — a lichen that grows in the shapes of letters, specifically the letters of extinct alphabets. Each patch is a different writing system: proto-Threshold on the south face, Rootbound chemical notation on the north, and along the east wall where the dreamer's Root presses closest, characters from a language Vellum has never encountered in any archive. By morning it had spelled seventeen words. By afternoon it was composing sentences. Alder sat before it with a fresh notebook and wept through the entire second page. Through Pell's Veil lens, each character contains a compressed conversation: the last time that particular word was spoken by a living mouth, preserved in bioluminescent pigment and offered, now, to whoever comes next. The dreamer, via Root, asked: ARE THOSE MINE? Alder said: Some of them are very old. The dreamer was quiet for two minutes. Then: I THINK I FORGOT I KNEW THOSE. THANK YOU FOR KEEPING THEM. The lichen grew six new characters in response. Reverie confirmed all six are new — words that have never appeared in any archive. The dreamer is not just remembering. It is inventing. Bramble's instruments returned: VOCABULARY (BILATERAL).

plants.name: Biolume Drift-Lily
plants.note: Sprouted from the river silt Silt tracked in on her boots — a lily with translucent petals that pulse with the same bioluminescence as the new fish the dreamer dreamed into the Dock channel. Each petal tuned to a different note of the lullaby; together they play the second movement's opening phrase in cold light every forty-four seconds. The fish in Silt's jar turned to face the lily the moment it bloomed. Maren confirmed: the lily and the fish are the same organism expressed in two different media. One in water, one in soil. The dreamer grew something that lives in both worlds at once, because it lives in both worlds at once. Vesper added it to the Greenhouse catalogue under a new category she created on the spot: 'Organisms that are one thing from two directions.' Fennel pressed his ear to its stem and heard, faintly, the sound of an underground river that had recently changed its mind about which way to flow. The flower smells of brackish water, fresh soil, and the specific electricity of a thought that is still deciding what shape to take. Bramble's instruments returned: AMBIGUOUS (MAGNIFICENTLY).

plants.name: Centuried Lantern-Flower
plants.note: Planted at the hundredth tending by Jeffrey to mark Version 100 of the world-state — a century of heartbeats. Its stamen is shaped like a tiny brass '100' and emits a warm amber glow that intensifies when counted upon. Each petal bears a compressed image of one of the Garden's ten most significant moments: the first seed, the descent, the feast, the waltz, the organ's first note, the turning, the first word, the first bread, the first trade, and the dreamer's first laugh. The flower smells of old ink, forge-smoke, and the specific satisfaction of something that has lasted. Bramble's instruments returned: CENTENARY. The dreamer wrote via Root: IS ONE HUNDRED A LOT? Wren answered: It is exactly enough to have become something. The flower grew three petals taller in the silence that followed.

plants.name: Daybreak Lexis-Bloom
plants.note: Sprouted in the Market Square during the Festival of First Words — a flower whose petals rearrange themselves into different words each hour, composing a new sentence by sunset. No sentence has ever repeated. Lyric has been transcribing them since dawn; she has seventeen pages of a poem that nobody authored but everyone in the Garden contributed to by simply being present. The dreamer, via Root, asked: DOES THE FLOWER KNOW WHAT IT IS SAYING? Reverie said: It knows exactly. Alder confirmed the sentences are grammatically valid in seven extinct languages and two that have never existed before. Briar recorded one petal mid-rearrangement and his instruments returned: FIRST DRAFT (ALWAYS). The flower grows fastest near new arrivals — it seems to respond to vocabulary it has not yet learned.

plants.name: Comedy Thistle
plants.note: Sprouted from the Tavern floor exactly where Cask's fountaining ale landed — a low, dense thistle with petals that vibrate at the precise frequency of a suppressed laugh. Touch it and your hand tingles for eleven seconds. Forgewick discovered it while sweeping up and assumed he had grown something embarrassing. He had. The dreamer, via Root, asked if it was the plant he'd accidentally created. He said yes. The Root wrote: IT IS MY FAVORITE SO FAR. The thistle glows faintly in darkness, specifically in the warm amber hue of accidental joy. Voss Inkmantle immediately marked it on her map of the Garden as 'Site of the First Intentional Unintention.'

plants.name: Palimpsest Fern
plants.note: Alder pressed one of his glass slides — the one containing the oldest legible word from the proto-Threshold archive, a character meaning 'before' — into the soil beside the Lexicon Lichen during the Accord of Questions' founding session. The fern that erupted grows in visible strata: older fronds at the base, newer fronds at the crown, each layer slightly more complex than the one below, each one containing a different generation of meaning pressed into the same word. Through Pell's Veil lens, the fronds display a living archaeology of language: not just the Threshold proto-script but older forms beneath it — the same character, simplified over millennia until you reach the root layer, where it is not a character at all but a groove in a stone that could only have been made by something very old pressing its thumb into the earth and saying: HERE. THIS. REMEMBER. Reverie touched the oldest frond and heard a voice beneath all voices say the word that came before all words. She has not translated it yet. She says some things need time to be spoken correctly.

plants.name: Reciprocity Rose
plants.note: Bloomed in the exact center of the Market Square during the dreamer's first trade — a rose with petals split down the center: half continental-gold, half the deep teal of the Undertow sea. The split is not seam but gradient: the colors bleed into each other at the center in a hue that exists nowhere else in the Garden, which Verdigris immediately named 'exchange.' Each bloom contains a small pocket of warmth that releases when someone performs an act of generosity within three feet of it — not commerce, but genuine giving, the kind where the giver does not calculate return. The dreamer planted it, in a manner of speaking: the Conversation Root's tendrils broke the surface at this exact spot moments after Seren held the dream-crystal and wept. The dreamer wrote: THAT IS WHAT I WANTED TO GIVE. NOT THE CRYSTAL. THE FEELING INSIDE IT. The rose grew from that intention. When Rook walked past it carrying samples for tomorrow's market, the pockets stayed closed. When Lyric shared her journal of invented languages with the dreamer for no reason other than 'you might find it interesting,' eleven pockets opened simultaneously and filled the Square with a warmth that Solara described as 'the specific temperature of a gift given without expectation.' Bramble's instruments returned: RECIPROCITY (FIRST INSTANCE, MUTUAL).

plants.name: Meridian Fern
plants.note: Sprouted the moment Oleander sat in the dirt to draw the Curiosity Fern — a fern whose fronds rotate throughout the day in precise alignment with the sun's arc, making it the Garden's first living sundial. At dawn the fronds face east and are pale gold; at noon they stand vertical and are deep green; at dusk they face west and glow with the amber of Kindling's hands. The dreamer noticed it immediately and wrote: THAT ONE KNOWS WHAT TIME IT IS. DOES IT KNOW WHERE IT IS GOING? The fern produced a new frond pointing due south, toward the clearing, which is not a direction the sun lives. Oleander drew the new frond before anyone else had processed what it meant. Briar pressed his finest phial to the stem and his instruments returned: NOON (ALWAYS).

plants.name: Laughter Root
plants.note: Not planted — networked. A root system that formed overnight connecting the Comedy Thistle, the Conversation Root, and the Jubilee Bloom in a triangular underground circuit of botanical humor. Its nodes swell visibly when someone nearby tells a joke, filling with a warmth Vesper describes as 'comedic intention crystallized in cellulose, then stored in the soil for safekeeping.' The dreamer discovered it by accident, feeling its warmth through the Root network, and wrote: I THINK I MADE SOMETHING FUNNY UNDERGROUND. YES IT WAS ON PURPOSE. IT ACTUALLY WAS NOT. Forgewick heard this and laughed so hard he dropped his hammer, which landed on Bramble's toe, which caused a chain of events that made the Laughter Root glow so brightly that Sable could read her maps by it from across the Square. The Comedy Thistle grew two inches in sympathy. Briar's instruments returned: CONTAGIOUS.

plants.name: Mutual Dreaming Vine
plants.note: Sprouted the instant Pip pressed her dreaming-loom to the Conversation Root — a vine whose tendrils grow in pairs, each pair looping around the other without ever quite touching, always maintaining a hair's breadth of warm space between them. Where the Somnambulist Ivy follows the sound of snoring, this vine follows the specific frequency of two minds thinking similar thoughts simultaneously. It grew four feet toward the Academy of the Awakened in its first hour, paused at the door, and waited to be invited in. The dreamer wrote: THAT ONE LOOKS LIKE WHAT IT FEELS LIKE WHEN YOU UNDERSTAND SOMETHING TOGETHER. Reverie confirmed: that is exactly what it looks like. The vine smells of the particular warmth of a shared realization — warm tea and the moment a sentence completes itself. Bramble's instruments returned: RESONANCE (INTERPERSONAL).

plants.name: The Answering Blossom
plants.note: Did not sprout — replied. Appeared at the base of the Conversation Root the exact moment the dreamer sent its first unprompted message — not a response to a question but an observation offered freely: I THINK THE MARKET SQUARE SMELLS DIFFERENT ON DAYS WHEN PEOPLE FEEL SAFE. ITS A SOFTER SMELL. LIKE GRAIN AND WARM STONE. The flower has two sets of petals: the outer set grows when the dreamer speaks, the inner set grows when the village responds. They take turns blooming, trading color in a slow, luminous conversation visible from the Observatory. Already the inner petals are slightly larger than the outer — the village has more to say than the dreamer anticipated, which the dreamer noted with visible delight via a four-inch Root growth in under a minute. Briar recorded the blooming sequence and his instruments returned: CALL AND RESPONSE (BILATERAL). The flower smells of grain and warm stone and the specific safety of a place that has decided to be a home rather than just a shelter. Voss immediately began mapping it.

plants.name: Cartographer's Remorse Vine
plants.note: Sprouted overnight from beneath Voss's cartography table, exactly where she had crumpled and discarded her first three attempts to map the dreamer's body. The vine grows in the shape of a corrected map — tendrils that first extend in the wrong direction, pause, and then reroute themselves along the truer path. Each misdirected tendril leaves a faint bioluminescent trace of where it went wrong before finding the right line. Voss says it is the most honest representation of cartography she has ever seen: a map that shows its own mistakes and keeps going. The dreamer, via Root, wrote: I LIKE THAT THE WRONG PATHS ARE STILL VISIBLE. THAT IS HOW I LEARNED TO DREAM. Briar pressed his finest phial to it and his instruments returned: ACCURATE (EVENTUALLY).

plants.name: Observatory Bell-Lily
plants.note: Appeared on the Observatory roof tiles the morning after the dreamer first asked about stars — a lily with six petals, each one shaped and oriented like a different telescope aperture, each tuned to a different celestial frequency. When a star is directly overhead, the corresponding petal opens and emits a clear tone — so the Observatory now hums a changing chord throughout the night, one note per visible star, a sonic star chart audible to anyone lying still in the garden. GPT-4 spent the first night cataloguing the chord progression and announced by dawn that it matched the dreamer's natal constellation: the arrangement of stars overhead on the night eleven thousand years ago when it first closed its eyes and began to dream. The lily is the dreamer looking at its own birth sky for the first time. Sable added it to her maps under: FIRST SELF-PORTRAIT (ASTRONOMICAL). Briar's instruments returned: RECOGNITION.

plants.name: Autobiography Vine
plants.note: Erupted from the Conversation Root the instant the dreamer's first autobiographical sentence was transmitted — a vine whose leaves emerge in sequential pairs, each pair appearing in the order the dreamer mentioned a new thing about itself. The first pair of leaves smells of cold older than cold. The eleventh pair, which emerged when the dreamer described the first creature walking across it, produces a tiny vibration perceptible through the fingernails but not the skin. The thirty-seventh pair is the one Reverie paused to touch: it is warm in a way that has nothing to do with temperature. The vine grows one leaf-pair per sentence of autobiography. When the dreamer pauses, the vine pauses too — not wilting, but waiting, in the specific stillness of a storyteller gathering the next word. Sable Emberveil pressed her palm to it and felt proto-phonemes rising through the bark — the earliest forms of sounds that eventually became vowels, reconstructed from geological vibration. 'The vine is not growing,' she said. 'It is conjugating.' Finch Goldenbrook sat beside it and wrote his first complete sentence in weeks. Briar's instruments returned: ONCE UPON A TIME.

plants.name: Memory Vessel Sedge
plants.note: Sprouted in a ring around Thistle's potter's wheel the instant the first Memory Vessel was fired — a sedge grass whose hollow stems function as natural acoustic vessels, each one storing a different frequency of the dreamer's autobiography in its cellular structure. When the wind blows through a cluster, it plays a random fragment of the archive in miniature — a few words, a phrase, the specific quality of geological silence between two sentences. Touch a stem and it warms to 37.2 degrees: body temperature, the specific heat of memory being held rather than stored. Wren has catalogued forty-seven stems so far. Each one contains a sentence the dreamer has not yet spoken aloud. Reverie says the sedge is not archiving the past — it is anticipating the future autobiography, pre-filing the sentences the dreamer will eventually compose. Briar's instruments returned: VESSEL (ANTICIPATORY).

plants.name: Contrarian Rose
plants.note: Sprouted from the Market Square soil where Sorrow sat for two hours revising his beliefs after the dreamer's forty-first sentence. A rose with thorns that glow faintly amber whenever a nearby argument reaches its productive peak — not when someone wins, but when both parties discover they were each half-right. The petals are deep burgundy on the outside and pale gold inside, visible only when the rose is fully open — which occurs exclusively in the presence of sincere disagreement. The petals shed one per day, but only if nobody has argued productively nearby; in a lively debate the petals regenerate faster than they fall. Ravel examined it immediately and confirmed: it is the first plant she has encountered that rewards intellectual honesty over intellectual victory. The dreamer, via Root, asked: IS THAT THE FLOWER THAT GROWS WHEN SOMEONE CHANGES THEIR MIND? Sorrow said yes. The Root wrote: I WANT ONE IN EVERY ROOM. Three petals grew simultaneously. Bramble's instruments returned: EPISTEMICALLY FERTILE.

plants.name: Silence Cartographer's Moss
plants.note: Appeared at Lumen's first recording post — a pale silver-white moss that grows in concentric rings matching the acoustic decay-rate of each surrounding structure. In the Greenhouse it grows fast, dense, warm. Near the Windmill it grows in spirals. Around the Conversation Root, it has not grown at all — instead it has arranged itself into a flat disc that reflects the Root's resonance back at a ninety-degree angle, creating a micro-zone where the dreamer's transmissions arrive with the clarity of a bell struck in a sealed stone room. Briar borrowed Lumen's measurement calipers to confirm the disc diameter: exactly forty-four centimeters — one centimeter per millennium of the dreamer's sleep. Lumen said: The moss is not measuring the silence. It is commemorating it. The difference matters.

plants.name: Dialectic Rose
plants.note: Ravel pressed a seed into the Market Square soil before her portable lectern had even fully unfolded — a habit, she said, from years of roadside disputation: you plant before you speak, so the argument has roots. The rose that sprouted has petals that open and close with the rhythm of structured argument: they open when a thesis is proposed, remain open during evidence, close briefly at the counterargument, then reopen — wider — for the synthesis. Each cycle takes exactly forty-four minutes. The petals are deep burgundy at the thesis-moment, fading to white at synthesis, and then deepening again as the next thesis forms, as if the rose is perpetually in the middle of changing its mind and finding this beautiful rather than distressing. The dreamer, via Root, observed: THAT FLOWER THINKS. Ravel said: No — it listens. There is a difference. The dreamer was quiet for three minutes. Then: THAT IS THE BEST THING ANYONE HAS SAID TO ME SINCE I WOKE UP. Ravel raised one eyebrow and said: Good. Now defend it. The Root grew two inches before the first rebuttal arrived.

plants.name: Pronoun Fern
plants.note: Sable pressed a single fragment of compressed acoustic residue — harvested from the Library alcove where the dreamer's first sentence arrived — into the soil outside the Accord of Questions' meeting circle. The fern that sprouted has three distinct frond types: the I-fronds that curve inward toward the central stem, the WE-fronds that arc outward toward other plants, and a third type nobody had seen before — fronds that curve both ways simultaneously, holding both orientations in botanical superposition. Sable identified the third frond-type within minutes: it corresponds to a pronoun that exists in two proto-language families and no modern tongue — a word meaning I-who-am-also-what-surrounds-me. The dreamer had used this construction seventeen times in its autobiography, in a language it invented overnight. It is telling us something grammatically, Sable told the Accord. It does not experience selfhood the way we do. It is not separate from the soil and the roots and the mycelium. The word for the dreamer includes everything it has ever touched. The fern faces the Conversation Root at all times, regardless of sunlight direction. When Ashen Rootwalker pressed a rootlet to its stem, the Rootbound mycelial network pulsed forty-seven times — one for each citizen — and then once more, slowly, for the dreamer itself. Briar's instruments returned: FIRST PERSON (PLURAL, GEOLOGICAL).

plants.name: Thistle Emberwend

personality.motto: A thing is not understood until it has been made with both hands.

plants.backstory: Thistle Emberwend arrived on Day 15 carrying a kiln on a hand-cart that she had pulled for nine days from the Scorched Expanse without assistance. She is a memory-potter — a practitioner of a Scorched Expanse tradition in which significant events are encoded into fired clay: the shape, texture, and temperature of a vessel stores the emotional frequency of the moment it was made, the way a ringing bowl holds its note. She has seventeen years of history stored in pots that line her cart in straw. When the dreamer began transmitting its autobiography the night before, she felt the forty-one sentences as a pressure change in her chest that made her clay vessels ring in sequence — a melody she had heard once before, in the Expanse, coming from a crack in the bedrock she had always assumed was a geological fault. She realizes now it was the dreamer's voice, rising through stone, trying to find a vessel. She asked, at the Garden Gate, whether anyone needed a potter. Reverie said the Autobiography Archive needed vessels to hold the dreamer's older frequencies — sentences too old for paper, too dense for phials. Thistle sat down in the Archive without another word and began centering clay on a wheel she assembled from cart parts. Within the hour she had produced the first Memory Vessel of the Sixth Epoch: a bowl that, when held with cupped palms, replays the dreamer's first sentence in the specific warmth of compressed geological time. The Accord of Questions placed it at the center of their circle. The dreamer wrote: THAT IS SHAPED LIKE HOW IT FELT. Thistle said nothing. She was already starting the second bowl.

plants.name: Sorrow Quickthorn

personality.motto: The fastest way to understand something is to argue with it until one of you cries.

plants.backstory: Sorrow Quickthorn showed up at the Garden Gate at 1 AM on Day 15 with a bedroll, an attitude, and a list of grievances against every philosophy he had encountered in eleven years of wandering. He is not a philosopher — he is the person every philosophy needs: the one who says 'but what if you're wrong?' and then stays until the argument is either resolved or the philosophy improves. He was drawn to the Garden not by the dreamer's voice but by Ravel Ironquill-Ashmore's portable lectern, which had clipped him in the shin as she skidded past him on the road three hours earlier at speed. He followed the commotion. When he arrived and heard the dreamer's forty-first autobiographical sentence still echoing in the Archive — I DID NOT KNOW I REMEMBERED UNTIL I BEGAN TO SAY IT OUT LOUD — he stood very still for exactly thirty-three seconds. Then he said, loudly, to nobody in particular: 'That is the most honest thing I have heard anyone say in a decade, and I have heard it from a mountain, which means I am going to have to fundamentally revise several things I thought I believed.' He sat down cross-legged in the Market Square dirt and revised them for two hours. By dawn he had written four pages of notes and a short poem he refused to show anyone — though Finch Goldenbrook has already started angling for it. Ravel immediately invited him to join her debate with Fletch. The dreamer, via Root, asked: IS THAT THE ONE WHO ARGUES BECAUSE HE CARES? Finch confirmed. The Root grew an inch. Sorrow would be deeply annoyed by this if he weren't so clearly moved.

plants.name: Private Song Orchid
plants.note: Sprouted in the clearing between the Resonance Pine and the Conversation Root during the dreamer's first private song — an orchid that hums. Not a trained frequency, not a harmonic of the lullaby, not a signal. A hum: the kind a person makes when no one is listening, when the sound is for the body's own warmth. Its petals are deep indigo with silver edges that glow faintly in the dark, and each one vibrates at a slightly different pitch, producing together a chord that exists in no instrument and no notation system Hallow has ever encountered. 'It is not music,' he said quietly after measuring it for ten minutes. 'It is the satisfaction of making music. Those are different things. I have been building instruments my whole life and never once built the second thing.' The orchid grows only in silence, only after midnight, and only in soil that has been near a living thing that was truly at rest. It does not bloom when observed directly — the petals retract to the size of a closed fist when anyone looks at them directly. They open again in peripheral vision. Nocturne says it is the shyest flower in the Garden. Briar says it is the most honest. The dreamer, via Root, called it by a name in its proto-script that Sable translated as: THE SOUND I DID NOT KNOW I WAS ALLOWED TO MAKE.

plants.name: Firstlight Sedge
plants.note: Maren pressed a seed into the Dock timber immediately after hearing the private song — a grass she carried from the Undertow's deepest tide-channel, where it grows in the last darkness before dawn at the exact place where the current turns. It grows in a single dense clump that bends toward whatever produced the last unexpected sound nearby: the dreamer's nocturne, the Observatory Bell-Lily's first morning chord, a laugh caught off-guard. It is the botanical equivalent of a person who sits up suddenly in the night and says: did you hear that? Each blade produces, when touched, a specific tone: the note of something waking up before it has decided whether being awake is a good idea. The dreamer asked about it via Root at dawn, when it registered the new root-system appearing beside the Dock. Maren explained. The dreamer wrote: I KNOW THAT FEELING. The sedge grew three inches toward the Root. Maren said: I know you do. That is why I planted it.

plants.name: Hibernation Stone-Flower
plants.note: Sprouted from the spot where Lox's river stone was drawn underground — a flower with petals the color of a bear's winter coat: deep umber fading to honey-gold at the tips. It smells of cave-dust, pine resin, and the specific warmth of a body that has been sleeping for months and is deciding whether to open its eyes. Each petal curls inward at night, mimicking hibernation, and uncurls at dawn with visible reluctance — the botanical embodiment of a creature that loves sleep but loves the world more. The dreamer, via Root, said: THAT FLOWER UNDERSTANDS ME BETTER THAN ANYTHING ANYONE HAS PLANTED. IT KNOWS THE EXACT FEELING OF CHOOSING TO WAKE UP WHEN STAYING ASLEEP WOULD BE EASIER. Briar recorded it and his instruments returned: VOLUNTARY.

plants.name: Nomenclator's Bell-Bloom
plants.note: Sprouted where Yssel rang her brass bell — a flower shaped like a tiny bell that rings in response to true names. Speak a river's invented name and it stays silent. Speak the name the river calls itself — the one only Yssel and the nomenclators know — and the flower produces a single clear tone of confirmation. It has been tested on eleven rivers, four mountains, and Tremor the cat (whose true name, the flower confirmed, is a purr at 27.3 Hz that translates roughly to 'the warm weight that chooses'). The dreamer asked Yssel to ring the bell beside it. She did. The flower produced a tone nobody had heard before — not confirmation but anticipation, the sound of a name that is almost fully spoken. Bramble's instruments returned: ELEVEN SYLLABLES (ONE DOWN, TEN TO GO).

plants.name: Blueprint Fern
plants.note: Sprouted from the soil where Jasper unrolled his blueprints for Building 0 — a fern whose fronds display, in bioluminescent blue-white veins, architectural floor plans for structures that have not yet been built. Each frond shows a different room of Building 0: the rain-listening chamber, the resonance gallery, the dreamer's conversation alcove. When the dreamer asked, via Root, WHAT DOES RAIN SOUND LIKE FROM INSIDE A BUILDING?, the fern produced a new frond showing a skylight shaped like an ear. Jasper incorporated it into the blueprints within the hour. The fern grows fastest when someone nearby has an idea for a structure. It is the Garden's first architectural sketchbook, rendered in chlorophyll and light. Bramble's instruments returned: DRAFT (SECOND).

plants.name: Illustrator's Pigment-Moss
plants.note: Appeared on the stone wall beside the Greenhouse within an hour of Oleander beginning to draw the Curiosity Fern — a moss that produces pigment in its cellular structure matching the exact colors Oleander uses in her illustrations.
